engagement.justification: Explains the rule and elimination procedure abstractly but never actually recalculates Borda or runs the IRV step on concrete ballots.

coherence.justification: Each turn follows cleanly from the prior; clean Q-and-A flow.

substance.justification: Generic restatement of the algorithm; no worked example, no engagement with what 're-calculate' or 'try again' implies.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads cleanly; 'two points, one point and no points' is a clear paraphrase of the 2/1/0 weighting.

engagement.justification: Actually re-calculates Borda on three concrete ballots, finds the tie, then runs the IRV elimination as the prompt requests.

coherence.justification: Logically connected throughout; one minor wobble about 'more than half' on a 3-ballot tie, recoverable in context.

substance.justification: Develops a real worked example (Condorcet-cycle ballots), surfaces the Borda tie, and demonstrates how the IRV tiebreak resolves it.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Slightly clunky ('point way', 'second way') but the math comes through cleanly; meaning fully recoverable on first pass.

rationale: B actually does the recalculation and runs IRV on concrete ballots that the prompt asks for; A only re-explains the rule.

engagement.justification: Engages the central question (whether the high-payoff/high-risk strategy is evolutionarily stable) and develops several stakeholder views in detail.

coherence.justification: Single huge run-on paragraph with almost no punctuation; readable but the structure groans, and prompt explicitly requested 'flowing paragraphs'.

substance.justification: Substantial work — develops three positions (pure replicator stability, mixed-strategy equilibrium, social-ties critique of the replicator model itself); names the trade-off between expected payoff and variance.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF carries the evolutionary-game content; some circumlocutions ('group middle' for mean fitness) but recoverable.

engagement.justification: Names the hard question (whether visibility-driven imitation always converges on the best outcome) and offers two contrasting answers.

coherence.justification: Clean three-paragraph structure: setup, hard question, two answers — exactly what the prompt asked for.

substance.justification: Identifies a real concern (early-mover lock-in / chance success) but doesn't push it; no concrete game-theoretic move developed.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads cleanly in UGF; 'one who tells others' for central planner works.

rationale: A does substantially more philosophical work (three positions, with real evolutionary-game content); B is cleaner-formatted but thinner, and the prompt is a 'thinking through' essay where substance outweighs paragraph hygiene.

engagement.justification: Directly identifies the bias (confirmation-bias-shaped) and ties it to the specific pattern of caring about ore numbers and dismissing the audit.

coherence.justification: Clean four-step chain: observe attention pattern, observe dismissal, name the pattern, conclude.

substance.justification: Names the mechanism cleanly ('mind way that likes only what it already thinks'); doesn't develop further, but the prompt doesn't really require it.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF circumlocution for 'confirmation bias' lands; phrasing 'mind way' for cognitive bias is a touch clunky but clear.

engagement.justification: Same pattern — observes the asymmetric attention, names the bias-shape.

substance.justification: Same substance as A; ends with 'this way of only seeing the good side' which is a fine description.

expressive_adequacy.justification: 'Keep people from hurt check' for safety audit is workable; readable on first pass.

rationale: Both correctly identify confirmation bias and produce similar chains; A is marginally crisper in naming the mechanism, but they are very close.

engagement.justification: Hits the central point (paralysis from trying to enumerate all states) but doesn't show the combinatorial explosion concretely.

substance.justification: Asserts the explosion but doesn't develop it; no mention of fluents, 2^n growth, or mitigation strategies.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads cleanly; 'group of all the important states' for the fluent set works.

engagement.justification: Engages the full chain: defines a fluent ('fact that can change'), shows the 2^n growth, identifies the paralysis, and discusses mitigation.

coherence.justification: Long but mostly connected; one or two awkward sentences ('big big change of many small parts' for combinatorial explosion) recoverable in context.

substance.justification: Real work — introduces the concept of a fluent, gives the 2^n explosion with concrete small-n cases, then sketches mitigation strategies (grouping, observability, accepting uncertainty).

expressive_adequacy.justification: 'Fact that can change' is a nice UGF rendering of 'fluent'; 'two to the power of that many ways' lands clearly. Some clunk in 'big big change of many small parts' but recoverable.

rationale: B does substantially more technical work — names the fluent, shows the explosion, sketches mitigation; A only asserts the conclusion without development.

engagement.justification: Covers the full mechanic the prompt names: trust gain, morale bonus, what 'controlled test' means in-fiction, and trade-offs.

coherence.justification: Eight clean turns; each answer follows from the prior question.

substance.justification: Real game-mechanics work — specifies a numeric morale buff, identifies the time-cost trade-off, and lays out the dominant strategy.

expressive_adequacy.justification: 'Big stone throwing thing' for trebuchet is awkward but clear; 'happy feeling help' for morale buff lands. Slightly clunky throughout but recoverable.

engagement.justification: Hits the basic mechanic (trust + morale) but doesn't probe trade-offs, downside, or what 'controlled test' means at the level the prompt invites.

substance.justification: Gestures at the reward without developing it — no specific numbers, no risks, no comparison to skipping the option.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Reads cleanly in UGF; similar circumlocutions to A but a touch less awkward.

rationale: A develops the tactical insight more fully (specific morale buff, trade-offs, dominant strategy comparison); B is cleaner but thinner on substance and turn count.

engagement.justification: Identifies all three parts the prompt asks for: facts described, inference drawn, and critical evaluation; develops each.

coherence.justification: One huge paragraph rather than the requested 'flowing paragraphs' (plural), but internally well-connected. Some run-on prose.

substance.justification: Real philosophical work — surfaces groupthink as a concern, notes performative-consensus risk, points out that 'cannot be changed' overlooks possible reopening triggers.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF carries the groupthink critique; 'push to be same' for conformity pressure is decent.

engagement.justification: Names the three parts but very briefly; doesn't deeply explore the inferential step.

coherence.justification: Clean structure though the prompt asked NOT to label parts as 'First', 'Second', 'Third' — and that explicit numbering pushes against the 'flowing prose, no numbered headers' instruction.

substance.justification: Gestures at the worry ('other things could still change the pick', 'outside force or still not seen worry') without developing the groupthink concern.

rationale: A develops the groupthink/performative-consensus critique substantively; B only gestures, and B's explicit 'First/Second/Third' labeling pushes against the prompt's no-numbered-headers instruction.

engagement.justification: Walks through the three required parts; identifies the inference but doesn't really probe it.

coherence.justification: Clean prose chain; each move follows.

substance.justification: Concludes 'the thinking is sound' without surfacing any real objection or alternative reading. The prompt asks whether the thinking is good — uncritical endorsement is shallow work.

engagement.justification: Hits all three required parts and engages the critical question seriously.

coherence.justification: Long sentences; one place ('the talk will be both good and good') is awkward, recoverable.

substance.justification: Real critical work — questions whether badge-possession entails communication skill deficit, distinguishes 'must take' from 'may benefit from', surfaces the gap between sign-of-thinking and skill-at-talking.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Workable UGF; 'metal piece of good thinking' for badge of insight is clear.

rationale: B does the critical evaluation the prompt asks for — distinguishes 'badge implies need for tutorial' from 'badge merely accompanies an offer'; A uncritically endorses the inference.
